skills.title: Sounding out words
skills.milestone: Blends letter sounds to read and spell short single-syllable words (c-a-t → cat), including common digraphs (sh, ch).
skills.description: Decoding and spelling CVC words and simple digraphs by sounding out.
skills.lessonIntro: Every word is made of sounds. When we say each sound and squish them together, the word appears — c… a… t… cat!
skills.workedExample: For 'ship': point to each part and say /sh/ /i/ /p/, then blend it fast — ship. 'sh' is two letters that make one sound.
skills.parentNote: Let them tap each sound with a finger before blending. Stretchy sounds (sss, mmm) are easiest to start with.

questions.prompt: Sound it out and build the word:
questions.hint: Say each sound: /c/ /a/ /t/.
questions.explanation: Blending /c/ /a/ /t/ makes ‘cat’ — that's decoding.

questions.prompt: Sound it out and build the word:
questions.hint: ‘sh’ is one sound made by two letters at the end.
questions.explanation: /f/ /i/ /sh/ blends into ‘fish’; ‘sh’ is a digraph.

questions.prompt: Which two letters make the sound /sh/?
questions.hint: Say it slow — ‘shhh’, like being quiet.
questions.explanation: ‘sh’ is a digraph: two letters teaming up to make one new sound.

questions.prompt: Sound it out and build the word:
questions.hint: Start with the /sh/ sound, then /e/ /l/.
questions.explanation: /sh/ /e/ /l/ blends into ‘shell’ — two consonants at the end (ll).

skills.title: Reading & understanding
skills.milestone: Reads a short familiar text and answers literal questions about who, what and where.
skills.description: Literal comprehension of a one-sentence text.
skills.lessonIntro: Good readers don't just say the words — they picture what's happening, like a little movie in their head.
skills.workedExample: Read: ‘The dog ran to the big red ball.’ Ask yourself: Who ran? (the dog) What did it run to? (the ball) What colour was the ball? (red).
skills.parentNote: After reading anything together, ask one ‘who/what/where’ question. Re-reading to find the answer is a real reading skill.

questions.prompt: ‘The cat sat on the mat.’ Who sat on the mat?
questions.hint: Look for the animal in the sentence.
questions.explanation: The answer is right there in the words — that's called reading for detail.

questions.prompt: ‘Sam ran to the blue boat.’ What colour was the boat?
questions.hint: Find the word just before ‘boat’.
questions.explanation: The describing word (blue) tells us more about the boat.

questions.prompt: ‘We went to the beach and found shells.’ Where did we go?
questions.hint: Which place is named in the sentence?
questions.explanation: ‘Where’ questions are answered by finding the place word.

questions.prompt: ‘Mia was happy because she won the race.’ Why was Mia happy?
questions.hint: Look for the word ‘because’ — the reason comes after it.
questions.explanation: ‘Because’ links a feeling to its reason — that's understanding, not just reading.

skills.title: Writing a sentence
skills.milestone: Writes a simple sentence starting with a capital letter and ending with a full stop.
skills.description: Sentence-level conventions: capital letter and full stop.
skills.lessonIntro: A sentence is a whole idea. It wears a hat at the start (a Capital letter) and shoes at the end (a full stop).
skills.workedExample: ‘the dog is big’ isn't dressed yet. Fix it: ‘The dog is big.’ — capital T, and a full stop after big.
skills.parentNote: When they write, cheer the capital and the full stop first — conventions stick fastest when noticed.

questions.prompt: Which one should start every sentence?
questions.hint: It's the big version of a letter.
questions.explanation: Capital letters tell the reader a new idea is starting.

questions.prompt: What belongs at the END of this sentence? ‘I like to run__’
questions.hint: It's the tiny dot that means ‘stop’.
questions.explanation: A full stop tells the reader the idea has finished.

questions.hint: Look for BOTH a capital at the start and a full stop at the end.
questions.explanation: A correct sentence needs its hat (capital) and its shoes (full stop).

questions.prompt: Which word should be a capital because it's a name?
questions.hint: Names of people always wear a capital.
questions.explanation: Proper nouns — names of people and places — always take a capital letter.

skills.title: Words we just know
skills.milestone: Reads common high-frequency words (the, was, said, they) on sight, without sounding out.
skills.description: Instant recognition of the most common little words.
skills.lessonIntro: Some words are tricky to sound out — we just learn to know them by sight, like knowing a friend's face. Words like 'the', 'said' and 'was' pop up everywhere!
skills.workedExample: Look at 'said'. If we sound it out we get s-a-i-d — but we say 'sed'! So we take a photo of it with our eyes: s-a-i-d says 'said'.
skills.parentNote: Sight words unlock fluent reading. Spot them together in books and on signs — speed and confidence matter more than sounding out here.

questions.prompt: Build the word we say as 'the':
questions.hint: It starts with a t.
questions.explanation: 'The' is the most common word in English — you'll see it everywhere you read.

questions.hint: It has an i hiding in the middle.
questions.explanation: 'Said' doesn't follow the sounding-out rules — we know it by sight.

questions.prompt: Finish it: 'We ___ going to the park.'
questions.hint: More than one person needs 'are'.
questions.explanation: 'We are' go together — little words like 'are' glue sentences together.

questions.prompt: Build the word we say as 'was':
questions.hint: It rhymes with 'buzz' when we say it!
questions.explanation: 'Was' sounds like 'woz' — another sight word our eyes just know.

skills.title: Capitals & full stops
skills.milestone: Writes sentences that start with a capital letter and end with a full stop or question mark.
skills.description: The rules that make writing readable.
skills.lessonIntro: Every sentence wears a hat and shoes: a capital letter at the start is its hat, and a full stop at the end is its shoes. Questions end with a curly question mark instead!
skills.workedExample: 'the dog ran home' needs its hat and shoes: 'The dog ran home.' — capital T, full stop at the end.
skills.parentNote: When your child writes at home, ask 'has your sentence got its hat and shoes?' — a playful self-check beats correcting.

questions.hint: Look for the capital hat AND the full-stop shoes.
questions.explanation: A capital at the start and a full stop at the end make a complete sentence.

questions.prompt: What goes at the end of: 'Where is my hat'
questions.hint: It's asking something.
questions.explanation: Asking sentences end with a question mark — it curls like an ear listening for the answer.

questions.prompt: Which word ALWAYS needs a capital letter?
questions.hint: Names of special places and people get capitals.
questions.explanation: Names of people and places are proper nouns — they always wear a capital.

questions.prompt: How many sentences: 'We ran. It was fun. Then we ate.'
questions.hint: Count the full stops.
questions.explanation: Each full stop finishes one sentence — three stops means three sentences.

skills.title: Numbers to 100
skills.milestone: Counts, orders and reads numbers to 100 and knows the number before and after.
skills.description: Counting sequence, before/after, and tens & ones.
skills.lessonIntro: Numbers go in a steady order, like houses on a street. Every number has one just before it and one just after it.
skills.workedExample: What comes after 29? Count up one: 30. Before 30? Count back one: 29. The tens digit only changes when we pass a ‘ty’ number.
skills.parentNote: Count aloud together past the tricky ‘…9 → next ten’ jumps (39→40, 69→70) — that's where kids pause.

questions.hint: Count up one more.
questions.explanation: ‘After’ means one step forward when we count.

questions.hint: Count back one from twenty.
questions.explanation: ‘Before’ means one step back — the number you just said.

questions.hint: Say them in order and listen for the gap.
questions.explanation: Filling gaps shows you know the counting order, not just the numbers.

questions.prompt: How many TENS are in the number 34?
questions.hint: The first digit tells the tens.
questions.explanation: 34 is 3 tens and 4 ones — place value gives each digit a job.

skills.title: Adding to 20
skills.milestone: Adds two numbers within 20 using counting-on and number facts.
skills.lessonIntro: Adding is putting groups together to find how many altogether. A clever trick is to start on the bigger number and count on.
skills.workedExample: For 3 + 5, start on the bigger one (5) and count on three: 6, 7, 8. So 3 + 5 = 8. Starting big means less counting!
skills.parentNote: Encourage ‘counting on from the biggest’ rather than counting both groups from one — it's faster and builds fact fluency.

questions.hint: Hold up 2 fingers, then 3 more, and count them all.
questions.explanation: Adding joins the two groups into one total.

questions.hint: Start at 6 and count on four: 7, 8, 9, 10.
questions.explanation: Counting on from the bigger number is quicker than starting at 1.
When you pass 10, keep counting into the teens.

questions.hint: Make 9 into 10 first (borrow 1 from the 7), then 10 + 6.
questions.explanation: ‘Make ten’ turns a tricky fact into an easy one: 10 + 6 = 16.

skills.title: Taking away to 20
skills.milestone: Subtracts within 20 using counting-back and part–part–whole thinking.
skills.lessonIntro: Taking away means starting with a group and removing some. What's left is the answer.
skills.workedExample: For 7 − 2, start at 7 and count back two: 6, 5. So 7 − 2 = 5.
skills.parentNote: Use real objects: build the first number, physically remove the second, count what remains.

questions.hint: Start at 5 and count back two.
questions.explanation: Subtracting removes some from the group; what's left is the answer.

questions.hint: Count back from 10: 9, 8, 7, 6.
questions.explanation: Counting back is subtraction in action.

questions.hint: Take away the 3 ones to land on a tidy ten.
questions.explanation: Subtracting to a ten (13 − 3 = 10) is a handy landmark.

questions.hint: Go back to 10 first (−5), then one more (−1).
questions.explanation: Breaking the 6 into 5 and 1 makes the jump easier: 15→10→9.

skills.title: Telling the time
skills.milestone: Tells time to the hour and half-hour on an analogue clock.
skills.description: O'clock and half-past on an analogue clock; comparing length.
skills.lessonIntro: A clock has two hands. The short hand points to the hour; the long hand points to the minutes.
skills.workedExample: When the long hand points straight up to 12, it's o'clock. When it points straight down to 6, it's half past.
skills.parentNote: Read clocks aloud through the day: ‘the long hand is up — 3 o'clock!’ Real routines cement it.

questions.prompt: When the long hand points to 12, we say…
questions.hint: Straight up means a whole hour.
questions.explanation: Long hand on 12 = a full hour, so we say ‘o'clock’.

questions.prompt: The long hand points to 6. This means…
questions.hint: Half way around the clock is half an hour.
questions.explanation: Long hand on 6 means half the hour has passed — ‘half past’.

questions.hint: Think about how long each one feels.
questions.explanation: Comparing time: a day is much longer than a minute or a second.

questions.prompt: It is 3 o'clock. What time is one hour later?
questions.hint: Move the hour on by one.
questions.explanation: One hour later, the short hand moves to the next number.

skills.title: Shapes & patterns
skills.milestone: Names common 2D shapes by their features and continues a repeating pattern.
skills.description: 2D shape recognition and continuing AB / ABC patterns.
skills.lessonIntro: Shapes have sides and corners we can count. Patterns repeat in a rule we can hear and see.
skills.workedExample: A triangle has 3 straight sides and 3 corners. A pattern like red, blue, red, blue… follows the rule ‘swap every time’, so next is red.
skills.parentNote: Hunt for shapes and patterns in the house — tiles, windows, stripes. Naming the rule out loud is the key skill.

questions.prompt: How many sides does a triangle have?
questions.hint: Count the straight edges.
questions.explanation: A triangle is a 3-sided shape — ‘tri’ means three.

questions.hint: Think of a window or a cracker.
questions.explanation: A square has 4 sides all the same length.

questions.hint: Say the pattern out loud and keep the rhythm going.
questions.explanation: The rule is ‘red, blue, swap’ — so red comes next.

questions.hint: The rule is two triangles then a square.
questions.explanation: This is an AAB pattern — after two triangles comes the square again.

skills.title: Counting by 2s, 5s and 10s
skills.milestone: Skip-counts forwards by 2s, 5s and 10s from zero, spotting the pattern in the sequence.
skills.lessonIntro: Why count 1-by-1 when you can jump? Counting by 2s goes 2, 4, 6, 8 — like hopping over every second stone. 5s and 10s jump even further!
skills.workedExample: Socks come in pairs, so count them by 2s: 2, 4, 6, 8 — four pairs is 8 socks, counted in one breath.
skills.parentNote: Skip counting is the doorway to multiplication. Count real pairs (shoes, gloves) by 2s and fingers by 5s and 10s.

questions.hint: Jump two more from 6.
questions.explanation: By 2s each jump adds 2: 6 and 2 more is 8.

questions.hint: One whole hand more.
questions.explanation: By 5s each jump adds a hand of fingers: 15 and 5 more is 20.

questions.hint: The tens digit goes up by one.
questions.explanation: By 10s only the tens digit changes: 30 becomes 40.

questions.prompt: 6 pairs of shoes. Count by 2s — how many shoes?
questions.explanation: Six jumps of 2: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 — skip counting counts pairs fast.

skills.title: Sharing & halves
skills.milestone: Shares a small collection into two equal groups and recognises one half of a whole.
skills.description: Fair shares and the idea of half.
skills.lessonIntro: Sharing fairly means everyone gets the SAME amount. When we split something into 2 equal parts, each part is called a half.
skills.workedExample: 8 strawberries shared between 2 people: one for you, one for me, again and again — 4 each. Half of 8 is 4.
skills.parentNote: Use real snacks: 'share these fairly between us' — then name it: 'you found half!'. Fairness intuition becomes fraction sense.

questions.prompt: Share 6 apples between 2 kids fairly. How many each?
questions.hint: One for you, one for me, until they're gone.
questions.explanation: 6 shared into 2 equal groups is 3 each — half of 6 is 3.

questions.prompt: Which picture shows a fair half of a pizza?
questions.hint: Halves must be exactly equal.
questions.explanation: A half means 2 parts the SAME size — unequal pieces aren't halves.

questions.hint: Share 10 between 2.
questions.explanation: 10 split into two equal groups gives 5 and 5.

questions.hint: Fair means both numbers match.
questions.explanation: A fair share always makes two EQUAL groups — that's what half means.

skills.title: Coins & counting money
skills.milestone: Recognises Australian coins and orders them by value, counting small collections of like coins.
skills.lessonIntro: Australian coins come in different sizes and values: 5c, 10c, 20c, 50c, $1 and $2. Fun fact: the $2 coin is SMALLER than the $1 coin!
skills.workedExample: Three 10c coins: count by 10s — 10, 20, 30. That's 30 cents.
skills.parentNote: Play shop with real coins. Sorting and counting coins builds place-value and skip-counting at once.

questions.prompt: Which coin is worth the MOST?
questions.hint: Dollars beat cents.
questions.explanation: $2 is 200 cents — the most valuable Australian coin, even though it's small.

questions.prompt: You have two 5c coins. How much money?
questions.hint: Count by 5s: 5, …
questions.explanation: 5 and 5 make 10 — two 5c coins equal one 10c coin.

questions.hint: Count them up and check.
questions.explanation: 10 and 10 make 20 — different coin mixes can make the same amount.

questions.prompt: An ice block costs 50c. You pay with $1. What change?
questions.hint: $1 is 100 cents.
questions.explanation: 100 take away 50 leaves 50 — change is what comes back after you pay.

skills.title: Living & non-living
skills.milestone: Sorts things into living and non-living and knows living things grow, eat and need water.
skills.description: Living vs non-living and the basic needs of living things.
skills.lessonIntro: Living things grow, need food and water, and can make more of themselves. Non-living things don't.
skills.workedExample: A tree is living — it grows and drinks water. A rock is non-living — it never eats, drinks or grows.
skills.parentNote: On a walk, play ‘living or not?’ Ask ‘how do you know?’ — the reason matters more than the answer.

questions.hint: Living things grow and need food or water.
questions.explanation: Fish and plants grow and need food/water; rocks and spoons never do.

questions.hint: Which one grows and drinks water?
questions.explanation: A tree grows and needs water and light — it's alive.

questions.prompt: What do ALL living things need?
questions.hint: Think what you and a plant both need every day.
questions.explanation: Every living thing — plant, animal, person — needs water to live.

questions.prompt: A dog is living because it…
questions.hint: Being furry is how it looks — what does it DO that shows it's alive?
questions.explanation: Living things grow and eat; colour and fur are just features.

skills.title: Everyday materials
skills.milestone: Matches an everyday object to the material it's made from and why.
skills.description: Materials (wood, metal, glass, fabric) and their uses.
skills.lessonIntro: Things are made from materials chosen for a reason — glass is clear so we can see through a window.
skills.workedExample: A raincoat is made of plastic because plastic keeps water out. Paper would fall apart in the rain.
skills.parentNote: Pick up objects and ask ‘what's it made of, and why that?’ Purpose-led thinking is the goal.

questions.prompt: What is a window usually made from?
questions.hint: You can see straight through it.
questions.explanation: Glass is chosen because it's clear and lets light through.

questions.prompt: Why are raincoats made of plastic?
questions.hint: Think about what a raincoat has to do in the rain.
questions.explanation: Plastic is waterproof, so the rain runs off instead of soaking in.

questions.prompt: Which material is best for keeping you warm?
questions.hint: Think of a woolly jumper.
questions.explanation: Wool traps warm air, which is why jumpers are made from it.

questions.hint: Could you fold it easily?
questions.explanation: Soft things (sock, leaf) bend; rock and metal are rigid and hard.

skills.title: Weather & sky
skills.milestone: Describes daily weather, seasonal patterns, and how weather changes what we do and wear.
skills.description: Observing weather and how it shapes our day.
skills.lessonIntro: Scientists watch the sky! Weather is what the sky is doing today: sunny, rainy, windy or cloudy. It changes what we wear, what we play, and what plants and animals do.
skills.workedExample: Grey heavy clouds this morning → we predict rain → we pack a raincoat. Checking the afternoon: it rained! Our prediction was right.
skills.parentNote: Make a one-minute weather report at breakfast — observe, predict, then check later. That's the whole scientific method in miniature.

questions.prompt: Grey, heavy clouds fill the sky. What's most likely coming?
questions.hint: Clouds are full of tiny water drops.
questions.explanation: Thick grey clouds hold lots of water — when the drops get heavy, they fall as rain.

questions.hint: What keeps you dry? What blocks the sun?
questions.explanation: We choose clothes and gear to match the weather — that's using observations to decide.

questions.prompt: Which season is usually the HOTTEST in Australia?
questions.hint: It's when we swim the most!
questions.explanation: Summer (December to February here) brings the hottest days — seasons are the year's big weather pattern.

questions.prompt: The flag is flapping hard and leaves are flying. What did you observe?
questions.hint: You can't see it, only what it moves.
questions.explanation: We can't see wind itself — scientists spot it by what it pushes around.

skills.title: Past, present & future
skills.milestone: Sequences events using yesterday, today and tomorrow.
skills.lessonIntro: Time moves forward: what already happened is the past, what's happening now is the present, what's coming is the future.
skills.workedExample: Yesterday I went to the park (past). Today I am at home (present). Tomorrow I will go to school (future).
skills.parentNote: At dinner, ask ‘what did we do yesterday? what's tomorrow?’ Sequencing words are the skill.

questions.prompt: The day that already happened is called…
questions.hint: It's already finished.
questions.explanation: Yesterday is in the past — it has already happened.

questions.prompt: The day that is coming next is…
questions.hint: It hasn't happened yet.
questions.explanation: Tomorrow is in the future — still to come.

questions.prompt: Which happens FIRST in a day?
questions.hint: Think about the very start of your day.
questions.explanation: Ordering a day: waking up comes before dinner and bedtime.

skills.title: Healthy & active me
skills.milestone: Identifies healthy choices and fundamental movements.
skills.lessonIntro: Our bodies stay strong when we move, eat good food, drink water and sleep well.
skills.workedExample: Running makes your heart beat fast and strong — that's exercise looking after your heart.
skills.parentNote: Name the ‘why’ behind healthy habits — kids follow reasons better than rules.

questions.hint: Which one grows on a tree?
questions.explanation: Fruit gives your body energy and vitamins to grow.

questions.prompt: What should you drink lots of on a hot day?
questions.hint: It's clear and your body is mostly made of it.
questions.explanation: Water keeps your body cool and working well.

questions.hint: Which one gets you puffed?
questions.explanation: Active play exercises your heart, which is a muscle.

skills.title: Colour & making
skills.milestone: Recognises colours and how two colours mix to make another.
skills.description: Colour knowledge and simple colour mixing.
skills.lessonIntro: Artists mix colours to make new ones. Some colours are ‘primary’ — you can't mix them, but you can mix WITH them.
skills.workedExample: Blue and yellow mixed together make green — like leaves and grass.
skills.parentNote: Try real mixing with paint or coloured water — prediction then test is great thinking.

questions.prompt: Tap the colour of the sky on a sunny day.
questions.hint: Look up on a clear day.
questions.explanation: A clear daytime sky is blue.

questions.hint: Think of grass and leaves.
questions.explanation: Blue + yellow = green, one of the first mixes artists learn.

questions.hint: Think of a sunset or an orange fruit.
questions.explanation: Red + yellow = orange — warm colours making a warm colour.

skills.title: Rhyming words
skills.milestone: Hears and matches words that rhyme.
skills.lessonIntro: Rhyming words end with the same sound, like cat and hat. Listen to the ending!
skills.workedExample: Cat… hat. Say them slowly — they both end in ‘-at’. That's a rhyme!
skills.parentNote: Sing rhyming songs and pause for them to fill the rhyme — ‘the cat sat on the…’.

questions.hint: Which one ends with the same ‘-at’ sound?
questions.explanation: Cat and hat both end in ‘-at’ — that's rhyming.

questions.hint: Listen to the ending sound ‘-ar’.
questions.explanation: Star and car share the ending ‘-ar’.

questions.hint: Both end with ‘-ell’.
questions.explanation: Bell and shell rhyme on ‘-ell’.

skills.title: First sounds
skills.milestone: Identifies the beginning sound of a spoken word.
skills.lessonIntro: Every word starts with a sound. ‘Sun’ starts with /s/ — sssun.
skills.workedExample: Say ‘ball’ slowly: /b/… all. The first sound is /b/.
skills.parentNote: Play ‘I spy with my little ear, something starting with /m/’ — sounds, not letter names.

questions.prompt: What sound does APPLE start with?
questions.hint: Say ‘apple’ slowly and listen to the very start.
questions.explanation: Apple begins with the /a/ sound.

questions.prompt: What sound does FISH start with?
questions.hint: Stretch the first sound: ffffish.
questions.explanation: Fish begins with /f/.

questions.hint: Say each one and listen for /b/ at the start.
questions.explanation: Ball begins with /b/ — bbball.

skills.title: Letters in my name
skills.milestone: Recognises and names some letters, especially in their own name.
skills.lessonIntro: Letters have names and shapes. The letters in your name are the most special ones to learn first.
skills.workedExample: The letter M looks like two mountains. Can you find all the M's?
skills.parentNote: Point out the first letter of their name everywhere you see it — signs, books, cereal boxes.

questions.hint: A has a pointy top like a tent.
questions.explanation: Spotting the same letter in different spots is real letter knowledge.

questions.prompt: Which one is the letter M?
questions.hint: M has two humps like two mountains.
questions.explanation: M and W can look similar — M's points go up.

questions.hint: S is a curvy snake shape.
questions.explanation: S wiggles like a snake — sssss.

skills.title: Clapping the beats
skills.milestone: Claps and counts the syllables (beats) in familiar words.
skills.lessonIntro: Every word has beats inside it, like a little drum. 'Cat' has one beat: CAT. 'Ba-na-na' has three! We clap once for every beat.
skills.workedExample: Say 'el-e-phant' slowly and clap each part: el (clap) e (clap) phant (clap) — three claps, three beats!
skills.parentNote: Clap out family words, foods and animal names together. Hearing syllables now makes reading long words easier later.

questions.prompt: Clap it out: 'dog'. How many beats?
questions.hint: Say it and clap: DOG.
questions.explanation: 'Dog' is one beat — one clap.

questions.prompt: Clap it out: 'ap-ple'. How many beats?
questions.hint: AP … PLE.
questions.explanation: 'Apple' breaks into ap-ple — two claps.

questions.hint: Try clapping each one.
questions.explanation: Ba-na-na claps three times; cat is one and ti-ger is two.

skills.title: Counting to 10
skills.milestone: Counts a group of up to 10 objects with one number per object.
skills.lessonIntro: When we count, we say one number for each thing, and the last number tells how many.
skills.workedExample: Count 3 shells: point to each — one, two, three. There are 3 shells.
skills.parentNote: Count real things and have them touch each one — skipping or double-counting is the thing to gently fix.

questions.prompt: Tap each apple as you count them.
questions.hint: One tap, one number.
questions.explanation: Saying one number per object is how counting works.

questions.hint: Touch each star and count.
questions.explanation: The last number you say is the total — four.

questions.hint: Count slowly, one for each.
questions.explanation: Careful one-to-one counting gives five.

skills.title: Sorting & patterns
skills.milestone: Sorts by one feature and continues a simple AB pattern.
skills.description: Sorting by attribute and continuing patterns.
skills.lessonIntro: We can put things into groups that are the same, and we can make patterns that repeat.
skills.workedExample: Red, blue, red, blue… what's next? The pattern repeats, so red comes next.
skills.parentNote: Sort the washing or the blocks together — ‘why did you put those together?’

questions.hint: Say the colours out loud in a beat.
questions.explanation: The pattern swaps red/blue, so red is next.

questions.prompt: Sort them: round or not round?
questions.hint: Which ones could roll?
questions.explanation: Round things (ball, apple) roll; flat/pointy ones don't.

questions.hint: The rule is two yellows then a blue.
questions.explanation: This AAB pattern repeats: after two yellows, the blue returns.

skills.title: Figuring things out
skills.milestone: Solves a simple everyday problem by thinking it through.
skills.lessonIntro: When something is a puzzle, we can stop, think, and try an idea.
skills.workedExample: If 2 friends want to share 4 grapes fairly, give one each, then one each again — 2 each!
skills.parentNote: Pose little fairness and ‘what if’ problems in play; praise the thinking, not just the answer.

questions.prompt: You have 2 hands. How many gloves do you need?
questions.hint: One glove for each hand.
questions.explanation: Matching one glove to each hand gives two.

questions.prompt: Share 4 grapes between 2 friends. How many each?
questions.hint: Give one to each, then again.
questions.explanation: Fair sharing splits 4 into 2 and 2.

questions.prompt: It's raining. What do you take outside?
questions.hint: What keeps the rain off you?
questions.explanation: Choosing the umbrella shows you matched the tool to the problem.

skills.title: Shapes around me
skills.curriculumCode: QKLG: Active learning — early mathematical ideas (shape)
skills.milestone: Names circles, squares, triangles and rectangles and spots them in everyday things.
skills.description: Naming shapes and finding them in the world.
skills.lessonIntro: Shapes are everywhere! A wheel is a circle, a window can be a square, a slice of watermelon is like a triangle. Shape-spotters see them all day long.
skills.workedExample: Look at a door: it has 2 long sides and 2 short sides with square corners — that's a rectangle!
skills.parentNote: Play shape I-spy on walks and drives. Naming shapes in real objects is stronger learning than flashcards.

questions.prompt: A ball looks like which shape?
questions.hint: It's round with no corners.
questions.explanation: A ball's outline is a circle — perfectly round, no corners to bump.

questions.prompt: How many sides does a triangle have?
questions.hint: Tri- means three!
questions.explanation: A triangle always has 3 straight sides and 3 pointy corners.

questions.hint: Round with no corners.
questions.explanation: You found every circle — round shapes with no corners at all.

skills.title: Big, small, more, less
skills.milestone: Compares objects and groups: bigger/smaller, longer/shorter, more/fewer.
skills.lessonIntro: We compare things all day: who is taller? which cup has more? Comparing means looking carefully at two things and saying what's different.
skills.workedExample: Two towers of blocks: yours has 6, mine has 4. Stand them side by side — yours is taller AND has more!
skills.parentNote: Comparing language (more, fewer, longer, heavier) is early measurement. Bath time and snack time are perfect practice.

questions.hint: Think about them side by side.
questions.explanation: An elephant is much bigger — comparing means picturing them together.

questions.prompt: Plate A has 5 grapes. Plate B has 2. Which has MORE?
questions.hint: 5 or 2 — which counts further?
questions.explanation: 5 comes after 2 when we count, so 5 grapes is more.

questions.prompt: Which is LONGER: a bus or a bike?
questions.hint: Which takes more steps to walk past?
questions.explanation: A bus stretches much further along the road — that's what longer means.

skills.title: All about me
skills.curriculumCode: QKLG: Identity — building a sense of self
skills.milestone: Talks about themselves, their feelings and things they can do.
skills.lessonIntro: You are you — with things you love, things you're learning, and feelings that are all okay.
skills.workedExample: ‘I can put on my own shoes’ — noticing what you can do builds confidence.
skills.parentNote: Name their growing independence out loud: ‘you did that all by yourself!’

questions.hint: Which face is smiling?
questions.explanation: Naming feelings from faces is the start of understanding emotions.

questions.prompt: You tried something hard and did it. You feel…
questions.hint: How do you feel when you succeed?
questions.explanation: Doing hard things gives us a proud feeling — that's confidence.

questions.hint: Which face has tears?
questions.explanation: It's okay to feel sad — spotting it helps us talk about it.

skills.title: Sharing & our world
skills.milestone: Takes turns, shares, and cares for living things.
skills.description: Turn-taking, kindness and caring for nature.
skills.lessonIntro: We belong to groups — family, kindy, friends. Taking turns and caring keeps everyone happy.
skills.workedExample: If we both want the ball, we take turns — you have it, then I have it.
skills.parentNote: Notice and name kind, sharing moments as they happen.

questions.prompt: Two friends want one toy. The kind thing is to…
questions.hint: How can you both get a go?
questions.explanation: Taking turns is fair and keeps friends happy.

questions.prompt: How do we care for a plant?
questions.hint: What do living things need?
questions.explanation: Plants are living and need water to grow — caring is a kind action.

questions.hint: Where do we keep our world clean?
questions.explanation: Putting rubbish in the bin cares for our world and animals.

skills.title: People who help us
skills.curriculumCode: QKLG: Connectedness — showing respect for diversity & community
skills.milestone: Names community helpers (doctors, firefighters, teachers) and what they do for us.
skills.lessonIntro: Our community is full of helpers! Doctors help us get better, firefighters keep us safe, teachers help us learn, and rubbish collectors keep our streets clean.
skills.workedExample: If someone is hurt, who do we call? An ambulance! The paramedics inside are helpers whose whole job is making people safe.
skills.parentNote: Wave to and name the helpers you see out and about. Feeling connected to community is a QKLG cornerstone.

questions.hint: You visit them for a check-up.
questions.explanation: Doctors and nurses are the helpers who look after our health.

questions.hint: They ride a big red truck.
questions.explanation: Firefighters rush to fires and keep everyone safe — brave community helpers.

questions.prompt: Match the helper to their ride!
questions.hint: Who uses a hose? Who carries a stretcher?
questions.explanation: Each helper has special tools and a special vehicle for their job.

skills.title: My moving body
skills.milestone: Shows growing control in fine and gross motor tasks.
skills.lessonIntro: Big muscles help us run and jump; little muscles help us hold a pencil and do up buttons.
skills.workedExample: Doing up a button uses your little finger muscles working together carefully.
skills.parentNote: Threading, playdough and drawing all build the fine-motor strength for writing.

questions.prompt: Which do you use your BIG muscles for?
questions.hint: Which one uses your whole body?
questions.explanation: Jumping uses big (gross-motor) muscles; the others use little ones.

questions.prompt: Which needs careful LITTLE finger muscles?
questions.hint: Which one is fiddly and small?
questions.explanation: Buttons need fine-motor control — the same muscles as writing.

questions.hint: How do we get our body ready?
questions.explanation: A little warm-up gets muscles ready and prevents hurts.

skills.title: Naming things
skills.milestone: Points to and names familiar animals and objects.
skills.lessonIntro: Everything has a name! Let's find and name things we know.
skills.workedExample: This is a fish. Can you find the fish? Point to it!
skills.parentNote: Name things all day — ‘there's the dog!’ — and pause for them to try the word.

questions.hint: The one that swims.
questions.explanation: Naming familiar things grows early vocabulary.

questions.hint: The one you can eat.
questions.explanation: Matching a word to a picture is early language.

questions.hint: The one that says quack.
questions.explanation: Ducks say quack — connecting words and things.

skills.title: Animal sounds
skills.milestone: Matches familiar animals to the sounds they make.
skills.lessonIntro: Animals make sounds! A dog says woof, a cow says moo.
skills.workedExample: Moo! Who says moo? The cow says moo.
skills.parentNote: Sing Old MacDonald and let them supply the animal sound.

questions.hint: The big farm animal.
questions.explanation: Cows say moo — matching sound to animal.

questions.hint: Your furry friend.
questions.explanation: Dogs say woof.

questions.hint: The one at the pond.
questions.explanation: Ducks say quack.

skills.title: My colours
skills.milestone: Points to and names familiar colours in play.
skills.lessonIntro: Colours are everywhere — red apples, yellow sun, blue sky! Naming colours helps us tell people exactly what we see.
skills.workedExample: Point at a banana: 'yellow!' Point at the grass: 'green!' Every colour has its own name.
skills.parentNote: Name colours during play and meals ('your red cup!'). Repetition in real moments beats drilling.

questions.hint: Like the sun!
questions.explanation: Bananas are yellow — same as the sunshine.

questions.hint: Look outside at the lawn.
questions.explanation: Grass and leaves are green — nature's favourite colour.

questions.hint: Red like a fire truck.
questions.explanation: The apple and strawberry are red — you spotted them!

skills.title: What happens next?
skills.milestone: Shows understanding of simple cause and effect.
skills.lessonIntro: When we do something, something happens back — that's cause and effect!
skills.workedExample: If you drop a ball, it bounces. If you splash water, it goes everywhere!
skills.parentNote: Narrate cause and effect in play — ‘you pushed it, and it rolled!’

questions.hint: The light one that sits on top.
questions.explanation: Light things float; heavy things sink — early science.

questions.hint: The heavy one that drops down.
questions.explanation: A heavy rock sinks to the bottom.

questions.hint: The really big animal.
questions.explanation: Comparing big and small is early thinking.

skills.title: Counting 1, 2, 3
skills.milestone: Counts up to three objects.
skills.lessonIntro: Let's count! One… two… three!
skills.workedExample: Two apples: one, two. There are two!
skills.parentNote: Count everything in twos and threes — steps, ducks, blocks.

questions.hint: One tap each.
questions.explanation: One number per apple — that's counting.

questions.hint: Point and count them.
questions.explanation: One, two, three — there are three.

questions.hint: Just count the fish you see.
questions.explanation: There's only one fish — that's ‘1’.

skills.title: Big and small
skills.milestone: Uses 'big' and 'small' to describe and compare familiar things.
skills.lessonIntro: Some things are BIG like a truck, and some are small like a pea. Let's find big and small everywhere!
skills.workedExample: Daddy's shoe next to your shoe: daddy's is BIG, yours is small — and they're both shoes!
skills.parentNote: Compare pairs at home (spoons, shoes, cups). 'Big and small' is your toddler's first measurement idea.

questions.hint: Which one could you sit on?
questions.explanation: The elephant is the big one — the mouse is tiny!

questions.hint: Which could hide under your finger?
questions.explanation: An ant is so small it can hide under one finger.

questions.prompt: Which is BIGGER: a house or a chair?
questions.hint: Which one do you go inside?
questions.explanation: A house is big enough to hold lots of chairs inside it!

skills.title: Moving my body
skills.milestone: Names big body movements and body parts.
skills.lessonIntro: Our bodies can do so much — jump, clap, stomp and wave!
skills.workedExample: Can you jump? Bend your knees and… jump!
skills.parentNote: Play movement games — ‘can you touch your toes? can you jump?’

questions.hint: Put them together — clap clap!
questions.explanation: We clap with our hands.

questions.hint: The part at the bottom.
questions.explanation: We walk on our feet.

questions.hint: The part you look with.
questions.explanation: We see with our eyes.

skills.title: Big feelings
skills.milestone: Recognises happy and sad feelings.
skills.lessonIntro: Feelings are okay. Happy feels like a smile; sad feels like tears.
skills.workedExample: This face is happy — see the big smile!
skills.parentNote: Name feelings gently as they come up — ‘you look sad, shall we have a cuddle?’

questions.hint: The smiley one.
questions.explanation: A smile means happy.

questions.hint: The one with tears.
questions.explanation: Tears can mean sad — and that's okay.

questions.hint: The one with closed eyes.
questions.explanation: Closed eyes and a yawn mean sleepy.

skills.title: Mine & yours
skills.milestone: Shows a sense of self and belonging.
skills.lessonIntro: You are special! You have your own name, your own family, your own favourite things.
skills.workedExample: This is your teddy — it's yours!
skills.parentNote: Talk about ‘yours’ and ‘ours’ and who's in our family.

questions.hint: The soft cuddly one.
questions.explanation: Knowing your things is part of knowing you.

questions.prompt: What do you sleep with at night?
questions.hint: The soft one for cuddles.
questions.explanation: Comfort objects help us feel safe.

questions.hint: The round one you throw.
questions.explanation: Choosing what to play with shows independence.

skills.title: Caring & nature
skills.milestone: Shows care for others and the natural world.
skills.lessonIntro: We share with friends and are gentle with animals and plants.
skills.workedExample: We give the plant a little water so it can grow.
skills.parentNote: Model gentle hands with pets and plants; praise sharing.

questions.hint: The green growing thing.
questions.explanation: Plants and leaves grow — that's nature.

questions.prompt: How do we help a plant grow?
questions.hint: Plants get thirsty too — water!
questions.explanation: A little water helps a plant grow.

questions.prompt: A friend is sad. The kind thing is a…
questions.hint: What makes someone feel better?
questions.explanation: A kind hug shows we care about others.

skills.title: Things that go
skills.curriculumCode: EYLF Outcome 2: Community (exploring the world around us)
skills.milestone: Names familiar vehicles and where they go (road, sky, water).
skills.lessonIntro: Brm brm! Cars drive on roads, boats float on water, and planes zoom through the sky. Everything that goes has its own place to travel.
skills.workedExample: A plane can't drive to the shops and a car can't fly — the plane goes UP in the sky, the car stays on the road.
skills.parentNote: Spot and name vehicles when out and about, and ask 'where does it go?' — road, sky or water.

questions.hint: Splash splash!
questions.explanation: Boats float — they travel on rivers and the sea.

questions.hint: Look up high!
questions.explanation: Planes have wings to fly way up in the sky.

questions.prompt: Tap everything that goes on the ROAD!
questions.hint: Wheels on the ground.
questions.explanation: Cars and buses roll on roads; boats and planes travel other ways.
